content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
July 13,
1994
Colleen C.
McAndrews
Bell,
McAndrews
& Hiltachk
1441 Fourth Street
Santa Monica,
CA
90401
Re:
Your Request for Informal
Assistance
Our File No.
1-94-215
Dear Ms.
McAndrews:
You have requested advice on behalf of Mayor Richard Riordan
concerning his duties under the conflict—of--interest provisions of
the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”).
1
As you were previously
advised,
we are treating your request as one for informal
assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c).
2
Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place.
In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us.
The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Ogeisby
(1975)
1
FPPC Ops.
71.)
In addition,
the
Commission’s advice is limited to the provisions of the Act.
QUESTION
May Mayor Riordan vote on the general annual budget for the
Metropolitan Transportation Authority
(“MTA”)
when it contains
authority for the expenditure of funds for contracts, purchases,
or projects, decided upon at prior meetings at which time he
disqualified from participating in the decision under Sections
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000-18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Informal assistance does not próvide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).)
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


File No.
1—94—215
Page
2
87100 or 84308?
CONCLUSION
Mayor Riordan may vote on portions of the budget and also
vote on the final budget provided the following procedures are
followed:
1.
The budget decision is divided into separate decisions
and the decisions in which Mayor Riordan has the disqualifying
financial interest are segregated from the other budgetary
decisions;
2.
The budgetary decisions concerning issues in which Mayor
Riordan has a disqualifying financial interest are considered
first,
and the final decision is reached as to those issues
without Mayor Riordan’s participation; and,
3.
Once final decisions have been made on those issues in
which Mayor Riordan has disqualifying financial interests,
Mayor
Riordan may participate and vote regarding other items in the
budget so long as those deliberations will not result in a
reopening or in any way affect the decisions from which Mayor
Riordan was previously disqualified.
4.
Mayor Riordan may vote on the final budget provided the
decision cannot result in a reopening or in any way change the
decisions from which Mayor Riordan was previously disqualified.
FACTS
Mayor Riordan sits as a voting member of the MTA by virtue of
his office.
The MTA is composed of the five Los Angeles County
supervisors, the mayor of Los Angeles, three other Los Angeles
city representatives,
four councilmembers from other Los Angeles
County municipalities, the director of CalTrans
(non-voting),
and
the executive officer.
You have provided general information about the legislative
process followed by the MTA.
First,
a project such as a subway or
light rail line is proposed by the staff.
Committees develop the
proposal and eventually the board debates the merits of the
project.
Prior to that debate,
board members assess whether or
Our previous advice letters have dealt with the segmentation of
decisions where a public official is disqualified under Section
87103.
We do not have sufficient facts to analyze the
segmentation of decisions where a public official is disqualified
under Section 84308.


File No.
1—94—215
Page
3
not they have financial interests that foreseeably would be
materially affected by such a project.
The next decision level
involves the selection of contractors to carry out the project.
This decision can occur months and years after the first policy
decision.
The final level of decision—making occurs every year
when funds are appropriated through development and adoption of
the annual general budgets.
ALYSIS
Section 87100 of the Act provides:
No public official at any level of state or
local government shall make, participate in
making or in any way attempt to use his
official position to influence a governmental
decision in which he knows or has reason to
know he has a financial interest.
Mayor Riordan is a “public official” as defined in the Act.
(Section 82048.)
Thus, he may not make, participate in making or
attempt to use his official position to influence a governmental
decision in which he has a financial interest.
Section 87103 provides that an official has a financial
interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally,
on the official or a member of his or her immediate
family or on other enumerated economic interests.
Secmentation
We have previously advised that a public official who is
disqualified from participating in certain decisions may,
nevertheless,
be able to participate in certain aspects of budget
discussions and decisions.
In many cases,
large and complex
decisions, like budget decisions,
4 may be divided into separate
decisions so that a public official who has a disqualifying
interest with respect to one component of the decision,
may
You have suggested that since the statutes passed by the
Legislature in 1990 provided remedies for violation of the
provisions of the Act by legislators but expressly excluded the
budget bill as a whole
(see Sections 87102,
87102.5 and 87102.6),
that approach should be adopted as the policy for all
jurisdictions.
However, there is nothing in the legislation
indicating an intent that these provisions apply to officials
other than members of the Legislature.


File No.
1—94—215
Page
4
participate in the other components.
5
(Torrance Advice Letter,
No.
I-92-359b; Reddoch Advice Letter,
No. A-92-336; Merkuloff,
Advice Letter,
No.
1—90—542.)
Where decisions are separable,
a public official may vote on
other components of the budget if the decision on the other
components will not have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on any of the public official’s economic
interests
the following procedure is complied with:
1.
The decision in which a public official has the
disqualifying financial interest is segregated from the other
budgetary decisions;
2.
The budgetary decisions concerning issues in which the
public official has a disqualifying financial interest are
considered first,
and the final decision is reached as to those
issues without the public official’s participation; and,
3.
Once final decisions have been made on those issues in
which the public official has disqualifying financial interests,
the public official may participate and vote regarding other items
in the budget so long as those deliberations will not result in a
reopening or in any way affect the decisions from which the public
official was previously disqualified.
Thus,
Mayor Riordan may be able to participate in decisions
and discussions of certain aspects of the annual general budget
provided the budgetary decisions in which Mayor Riordan has a
disqualifying financial interest are segregated and Mayor Riordan
follows the procedure described above such that he does not
participate in those aspects of the budgetary decisions in which
he has a disqualifying financial interest.
However, under some circumstances,
a series of decisions may be
too interrelated to be considered separately.
For example, where
there are alternative proposals for the expenditure of a portion
of the budget and a public official has a conflict of interest as
to one of the alternatives, the public official may not
participate in the consideration of the other alternatives because
it is interrelated with the alternative for which the public
official has a conflict of interest.
If, for example, the budget
allocates a portion of the budget for operating expenses,
and if a
vote to reduce the funds in this portion of the budget makes it
reasonably foreseeable that such reduction in funds will affect
the funds available for the alternative which resulted in the
public official’s disqualification, then the decisions are deemed
to be interrelated and the public official may not participate in
the decision concerning the operating expenses.


File No.
1—94—215
Page
5
Final Budget
Finally, the public official may also vote on the adoption of
the final budget, despite the fact that the final budget includes
items from which the public official was disqualified provided the
decision cannot result in a reopening or in any way change
6 the
decisions from which the public official was previously
disqualified.
7
(Cook Advice Letter,
No.
A-83—163.)
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at 916/322-5901.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Jeevan Ahuja
Counsel, Legal Division
SGC:JA:ak
6
An affirmative or negative vote of the final budget as a whole
is not deemed,
in and of itself, to “change” the decision from
which the public official was previously disqualified.
You have questioned what constitutes a “final” decision as that
term is used in the Cook Advice Letter,
No. A-83-163; in Cook the
Commission merely described the idea expressed in this paragraph
that a decision be reached as to those aspects in which the public
official has a disqualifying financial interest and subsequent
participation and decisions by the public official cannot result
in reopening or otherwise change the decisions from which the
official was previously disqualified.

87100 or 84308?

1.
